presentation.latex: SD-XL V AE [114, 115]. Then, all models are trained using a Smooth L1 loss with β = 1.0 on the Something Something V2 dataset [ 116], where we report the minimum validation loss achieved. In Figure 9 we report scaling results for the embedding dimension and non-embedding parameter count, as we found these axes behaved the most linearly. The results demonstrate that, despite achieving a higher init
presentation.unicode_plain: SD-XL V AE [114, 115]. Then, all models are trained using a Smooth L1 loss with β = 1.0 on the Something Something V2 dataset [ 116], where we report the minimum validation loss achieved. In Figure 9 we report scaling results for the embedding dimension and non-embedding parameter count, as we found these axes behaved the most linearly. The results demonstrate that, despite achieving a higher initial loss, EBTs scale at a more than 33% faster rate than the Transformer++.

evidence.verbatim_snippet: SD-XL V AE [114, 115]. Then, all models are trained using a Smooth L1 loss with β = 1.0 on the Something Something V2 dataset [ 116], where we report the minimum validation loss achieved. In Figure 9 we report scaling results for the embedding dimension and non-embedding parameter count, as we found these axes behaved the most linearly. The results demonstrate that, despite achieving a higher initial loss, EBTs scale at a more than 33% faster rate than the Transformer++.
